narrative:
Elise Stefanik has spent over a decade in Congress perfecting the art of looking like a reformer while cashing checks from the very forces she claims to oppose. Her career arc — from youngest woman ever elected to Congress in 2014 to Trump's fiercest House loyalist — is a masterclass in knowing which way the wind blows and sprinting to get in front of it.

On stock trading, Stefanik deserves rare credit: she signed the discharge petition to force a vote on banning congressional stock trading and claims she has never personally traded individual stocks during her tenure. But her enthusiasm for transparency evaporates the moment we move past that single issue. She has received nearly $980,000 from AIPAC-linked donors, with $204,000 flowing in during the 2023-2024 cycle alone — making the pro-Israel lobby her top funding source. Her top corporate PAC donors have included Blue Cross Blue Shield ($28,000 across campaign and leadership PAC), Amazon, AT&T, and Comcast, each contributing $10,000 in a single cycle. Her fundraising has ballooned from $3.1 million in 2016 to $15.3 million in 2024, a trajectory that suggests "draining the swamp" pays extraordinarily well.

On Pentagon accountability, Stefanik sat on the House Armed Services Committee and championed "defense wins for the North Country" — Washington-speak for funneling billions in unrequested defense spending to contractors in her district. She has shown zero interest in a full Pentagon audit, likely because the defense industry that funds her campaigns would prefer the books stay as murky as possible. The Pentagon has failed every single audit since they became mandatory in 2018, and Stefanik has not lost a minute of sleep over it.

She voted against certifying the 2020 election results even after the January 6th Capitol attack, yet positions herself as a defender of democratic institutions. On the DISCLOSE Act and dark money reform, she has been conspicuously silent — perhaps because anonymous donors have been quite generous to her political ecosystem. She introduced a paid family leave alternative backed by Ivanka Trump that offered families a child tax credit advance instead of actual paid leave — the legislative equivalent of offering someone a coupon when they need a paycheck.

Trump nominated her for UN Ambassador in 2025, only to yank the nomination when Republicans realized they could not afford to lose her House vote. She then launched a campaign for New York governor, only to drop out in December 2025 when Trump refused to endorse her — her retirement announcement conveniently landing the same day the Epstein files were released.

For a self-described champion of good governance, Stefanik has opposed virtually every structural reform that would actually limit the influence of money in politics, hold the military-industrial complex accountable, or give working families real support. She will ban her own stock trades, but do not dare touch her donor pipeline.

Dan Sullivan of Alaska is the kind of senator who calls himself a champion of fiscal responsibility and a defender of working Alaskans — all while sitting on a committee that oversees the EPA that repeatedly fined his family's chemical company, RPM International[1], where he held between one and five million dollars in stock[2]. Sullivan voted to confirm EPA administrators who conveniently rolled back regulations targeting RPM subsidiaries, and voted against amendments that would have strengthened chemical safety oversight. When asked about conflicts of interest, the senator's office reliably explained that he takes his ethics obligations very seriously.

The theme of loudly proclaimed principle quietly yielding to donor interest runs through Sullivan's Senate career like a familiar refrain. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act[3] — the bill that included Medicare's first real authority to negotiate drug prices — calling it a "socialist spending bill"[4] as pharmaceutical company lobbying money flows generously toward Republican campaign committees. Sullivan sits on the Armed Services Committee[5], sermonizes about Pentagon lethality and peace through strength, and yet has shown no appetite for the kind of financial audit of the Defense Department that might reveal where the trillions actually go. Apparently lethality is the core mission; accountability is someone else's department. His top industry donors, per OpenSecrets, include defense, oil and gas, and finance[6] — the trifecta of sectors that do very well when senators prioritize force projection over fiscal transparency and energy permitting over climate regulation.

On the transparency front, Sullivan's record is equally instructive. He was caught violating the STOCK Act in 2022, disclosing stock sales in Mowi — the world's largest farmed salmon company — and Five Below more than 45 days after the transactions, in one case 93 days late[7]. The excuse was that a third party managed the trades and Sullivan didn't know about them. For a senator who chairs no shortage of oversight hearings demanding accountability from others, the inability to monitor his own investment portfolio lands with a certain irony. He has shown no notable enthusiasm for banning congressional stock trading outright, an idea that polls above 70 percent with the American public. On the Epstein files, Sullivan first called for release, then voted in September 2025 to block a measure compelling DOJ to disclose them[8], then reversed course in November when the Senate passed a bill unanimously[9]. His position on government transparency appears to be: eventually yes, but only after a few strategic delays.

Sullivan did break from his party in April 2025 to vote against Trump administration cuts to Social Security services, joining only Lisa Murkowski and Susan Collins among Senate Republicans[10] — a rare flash of independence on a popular issue for Alaska's sizable senior population. He has also quietly benefited from dark money advertising, with a Virginia-based organization tied to a former McConnell aide running unofficial issue ads praising him as a health care leader[11] around the same time he was shaping legislation that his own office touted for its health care provisions. Whether this constitutes hypocrisy about dark money or simply successful dark money, the DISCLOSE Act — which would require donors to identify themselves — remains nowhere on his legislative agenda. Dan Sullivan is a senator who believes deeply in Alaska's resource economy, in families, and in the kind of transparency that applies to everyone except his own financial disclosures and the people writing his campaign checks.

Lloyd Smucker spent twenty-five years running a commercial construction company in Smoketown, Pennsylvania, before entering politics, which means he arrived in Congress in 2017 already fluent in the language of tax breaks, deregulation, and making sure the people who build things never have to worry about the people who cannot afford to live in them.

As a member of the Ways and Means Committee — the most powerful tax-writing body in Congress — Smucker chairs the Main Street Tax Team, a group dedicated to making permanent the Section 199A pass-through business deduction from the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. That provision lets owners of pass-through businesses deduct 20 percent of qualified income, a windfall that disproportionately benefits wealthy business owners like, well, Lloyd Smucker. His estimated net worth sits around $3.1 million, and he still holds a 40 percent interest in H2O To Go, a business that reported between $100,000 and $1 million in income in his financial disclosures. When your legislative priority is making your own tax break permanent, the phrase "conflict of interest" starts to feel quaint.

On January 6, 2021, Smucker voted to overturn Pennsylvania's Electoral College results even after the Capitol attack, joining 137 Republicans in objecting to Biden's certification. He later told constituents in a phone call that the January 6th committee testimony was "very credible," a fascinating admission from someone who voted to do exactly what the insurrectionists wanted. His campaign war chest, fueled by $614,000 in PAC money from the likes of Exelon and General Electric plus another $351,000 from individual donors including Lancaster County fixture S. Dale High, apparently suffered no consequences from his election denial.

The Problem Solvers Caucus, of which Smucker is a member, routed $277,000 in dark money through a veiled network of super PACs to support his campaigns — an ironic arrangement for a caucus that bills itself as the adults in the room. Smucker has shown no interest in the DISCLOSE Act, dark money reform, or banning corporate PACs that fund his political ecosystem.

To his credit, Smucker voted for the Epstein Files Transparency Act in November 2025, joining the near-unanimous 427-1 House vote. He framed it as a stand for "justice for the victims" and "accountability for those involved." It is always easier to demand transparency when 426 of your colleagues are doing the same thing.

On the 80% Bill's core planks — taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing tax loopholes, banning stock trading, auditing the Pentagon, funding Social Security — Smucker has been either actively hostile or conspicuously absent. He is a construction man who built his career on tax code loopholes, and he has no intention of sealing them shut.

Darren Soto is a Democrat representing Florida's 9th Congressional District, which stretches from the Orlando suburbs through Osceola and parts of Polk County, encompassing one of the most diverse districts in the state. A former Florida state senator, Soto was first elected to the U.S. House in 2016 and became the first member of Congress of Puerto Rican descent from Florida [1]. He sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee and the Natural Resources Committee [2], positioning him at the intersection of some of the 80% Bill's most consequential policy areas. Soto has cultivated a profile as a pragmatic progressive, though as with many Democrats in competitive-leaning districts, the distance between rhetoric and action can be instructive.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Soto has a substantially more supportive record than his Senate counterpart from Florida. He has co-sponsored the STOCK Act reforms and supported legislation to ban congressional stock trading [3]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — a provision that alone could save taxpayers hundreds of billions over the next decade [4]. He has co-sponsored the For the People Act (H.R. 1), which addresses fair elections, gerrymandering, and the DISCLOSE Act's transparency requirements [5], and he has backed the Protecting the Right to Organize Act and paid family leave legislation [6]. On ending forever wars, Soto voted to repeal the 2002 AUMF [7], siding with the bipartisan majority that believes Congress should not indefinitely outsource its war powers.

Where Soto's record grows thinner is on the issues that cut closer to the economic establishment. He has not been a leading voice on taxing the ultra-wealthy or closing the carried interest loophole, and while he has made supportive statements about banning corporate PACs and dark money, his campaign has continued to accept PAC contributions [8]. On the Pentagon audit, he voted for the National Defense Authorization Act without pushing hard for audit accountability provisions. He has supported police body camera legislation and the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act [9], and he has co-sponsored bills to protect consumers from junk fees [10]. On right to repair, he has shown some support through his Energy and Commerce work but has not been a primary champion. His record on banning corporate purchases of single-family homes and protecting U.S. farmland from foreign buyers is limited, though he has spoken about the housing affordability crisis in Central Florida.

The donor picture tells a familiar Democratic story. Soto's top contributing industries include lawyers and law firms, real estate, and the technology sector [11]. He has received contributions from health sector PACs and telecommunications interests [12] — industries directly affected by his Energy and Commerce Committee work on Medicare negotiation, junk fees, and right to repair. While his overall fundraising is modest by congressional standards, the presence of corporate PAC money in his campaign creates the same tension it does for any member who expresses support for banning such contributions. The telecom and tech industry donations are particularly notable given his committee jurisdiction over broadband and consumer protection policy.

Darren Soto is, by the scorecard, one of the more 80% Bill-friendly members of Congress — he aligns on Medicare negotiation, stock trading bans, election reform, AUMF repeal, paid family leave, police reform, and junk fee bans. But alignment in votes and co-sponsorships is not the same as leadership, and on the issues that would most disrupt the donor class — taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing carried interest, banning corporate PACs he still benefits from — Soto remains more fellow traveler than field general. The 80% Bill asks whether popular will can overcome entrenched money; Soto's record suggests he is sympathetic to the question but not yet fully committed to the answer.

Tom Suozzi is the kind of Democrat who makes progressives wonder if the party has an opposition or just a second fundraising arm for Wall Street. The Long Island congressman has built a career on fiscal moderation, bipartisan photo ops, and an uncanny ability to champion the interests of wealthy suburbanites while insisting he is fighting for the middle class.

His signature legislative crusade tells you everything: the SALT deduction cap. Suozzi became so singularly devoted to raising the cap on state and local tax deductions — a provision that overwhelmingly benefits the top 5% of earners — that Crain's New York Business dubbed him "Mr. SALT." He threatened to tank the entire Build Back Better Act unless the cap was raised from $10,000 to $80,000, an $85 billion annual tax break that even Bernie Sanders called a giveaway to the rich. He got his deal. The working families of Long Island got to watch.

On stock trading, Suozzi delivers perhaps the most breathtaking hypocrisy in Congress — and the competition is stiff. He co-sponsored the Bipartisan Restoring Faith in Government Act of 2025 to ban congressional stock trading while simultaneously being investigated for serial STOCK Act violations. He failed to disclose roughly 300 financial transactions between 2017 and 2020. In March 2025, he sold up to $50,000 of Global Industrial Co. stock that he had never publicly disclosed owning, exploiting a loophole in federal disclosure rules. The Foundation for Accountability and Civic Trust filed a formal ethics complaint against him in October 2025 for repeated violations. Co-sponsoring a stock trading ban you personally cannot follow is the congressional equivalent of a bank robber sponsoring a neighborhood watch.

His donor base reads like a who's who of industries the 80% Bill targets. Real estate developers have been his financial backbone since his days as Nassau County Executive — Home Depot co-founder Ken Langone, a top Republican donor, bankrolled his 2006 gubernatorial run. Clayton, Dubilier & Rice, a private equity firm, was the top donor to his 2024 Victory Fund. His campaign relies heavily on contributions from the real estate and insurance industries, with nearly half his fundraising coming from donations of $2,000 or more.

To his credit, Suozzi has supported Medicare drug price negotiation, voting to pass legislation out of the House Ways and Means Committee and advocating for expanding the number of drugs Medicare can negotiate. But one good vote does not erase a career spent carrying water for the donor class.

On the broader 80% Bill agenda — banning corporate PACs, auditing the Pentagon, closing dark money loopholes, banning corporate home buying — Suozzi has been largely absent. He represents a district full of people squeezed by exactly the forces these policies address, yet his legislative energy flows reliably toward the preferences of the people writing the big checks. "Mr. SALT" indeed.

Tina Smith's career arc traces the well-worn path from corporate boardroom to nonprofit virtue to political appointment, each step executed with the methodical precision of someone who earned an MBA from Dartmouth's Tuck School and spent her formative professional years in marketing at General Mills [1]. After learning to sell cereal to America, she opened her own PR firm, then pivoted to a stint as vice president of external affairs at Planned Parenthood of Minnesota — a role that, whatever its merits, proved an efficient pipeline to DFL politics. She served as chief of staff to Governor Mark Dayton, became his lieutenant governor in 2015, and was appointed to the U.S. Senate in January 2018 after Al Franken's resignation, winning the special election that November and a full term in 2020 [2]. In February 2025, she announced she would not seek reelection, citing a desire to spend time with her four grandchildren rather than, presumably, another six years of senatorial fundraising.

On the issues that animate the 80% Bill, Smith's record is genuinely substantive, if occasionally complicated by the fine print. She has been one of the Senate's most persistent voices on prescription drug pricing, co-sponsoring bipartisan legislation with Senator Kevin Cramer to cap insulin costs and penalize manufacturers for excessive price hikes [3]. Her provisions in the Inflation Reduction Act helped deliver the most significant climate investment in American history, including clean energy tax credits and funding for rural electric cooperatives [4]. She has pushed hard for mental health parity, introducing the Behavioral Health Network and Directory Improvement Act to crack down on insurance companies' "ghost networks" — those conveniently inaccurate provider lists that ensure your therapist is always somehow out of network [5].

The satirical footnote to Smith's pharmaceutical crusade is that she and her husband have held between $250,000 and $500,000 in Abbott Laboratories stock — a company with deep ties to the early marketing of OxyContin [6]. Publicly grilling Big Pharma CEOs while privately collecting dividends from the industry is the kind of bipartisan Washington tradition that transcends party affiliation, and Smith's financial disclosures have attracted the sort of scrutiny that makes "conflict of interest" feel less like an accusation and more like a job description. A Capitol Trades report flagged additional stock trades coinciding with her seat on the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee, raising questions about timing that Smith's office has not exactly rushed to answer [7].

Smith's committee work on HELP and Agriculture has produced real legislative wins for Minnesota, from expanding broadband in rural communities to championing tribal sovereignty through her seat on Indian Affairs [8]. She co-sponsored Medicare drug price negotiation, supported banning congressional stock trading — a position that reads differently given her own portfolio — and backed anti-dark-money reforms. Her voting record is solidly progressive: she opposed Trump-era tax cuts, voted for the Respect for Marriage Act, and was one of fifteen senators to support Bernie Sanders' resolutions blocking arms sales to Israel in 2025. Whether her retirement from politics represents genuine prioritization of family or a well-timed exit before the contradictions between her populist rhetoric and personal investments became a bigger story is, as they say in marketing, a matter of brand management.

Mary Gay Scanlon has built a career as the kind of Democrat who says all the right things about corruption, then quietly trips over her own ethical shoelaces. A former pro bono attorney at Ballard Spahr who spent fourteen years directing free legal services for low-income clients, Scanlon arrived in Congress in 2018 with genuine reformer credentials. The problem is that reformer credentials and actual reform are two very different things.

Take stock trading. Scanlon is a proud original cosponsor of the Ban Conflicted Trading Act, which would prohibit members of Congress from trading individual stocks. Stirring stuff. Except in August 2022, she was caught violating the STOCK Act after failing to disclose up to $110,000 in her husband's stock trades for nearly a year and a half. Her husband sold four stocks collectively worth up to $95,000 and swapped up to $15,000 in DuPont shares in February 2021, none of which she reported until August 2022 — roughly seventeen months past the 45-day deadline. She paid the standard $200 fine, the congressional equivalent of a parking ticket for insider-trading-adjacent behavior. She blamed it on her husband's financial advisor having discretionary control over the account, which is a fascinating defense from someone who wants to ban the very activity she failed to report.

On the Judiciary Committee, where she serves as Ranking Member of the Subcommittee on the Constitution and Limited Government, Scanlon has cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to drag dark money into the sunlight. She has also backed campaign finance reform and fair elections legislation. These are genuine positives. But her enthusiasm for transparency apparently does not extend to her own household portfolio.

Scanlon was carjacked at gunpoint in broad daylight at FDR Park in South Philadelphia in December 2021, an incident that became a national flashpoint when critics pointed out she had supported measures to redirect police funding. The episode was irrelevant to her policy positions but irresistible to her opponents, and she handled it with characteristic lawyerly composure.

Where Scanlon genuinely earns credit is on the structural reform front. She has supported Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, the PRO Act for workers' rights, and efforts to protect voting access. She sits on the Rules Committee, a powerful perch that lets her shape which bills reach the floor. On paper, she aligns with a majority of the 80% Bill's twenty planks.

But the gap between Scanlon's rhetoric and her personal compliance with existing ethics law is the kind of thing that makes voters' eyes glaze over with cynicism. You cannot champion banning congressional stock trading while your own household is blowing past disclosure deadlines. The $200 fine she paid would not cover a decent dinner in the Philadelphia suburbs she represents — and that is precisely the problem with congressional self-policing.

Elissa Slotkin's resume reads like a Tom Clancy novel ghostwritten by a centrist think tank. Before arriving in Congress, she spent three tours in Iraq as a CIA analyst, worked the Iraq portfolio on the National Security Council under George W. Bush, and served as acting Assistant Secretary of Defense under Obama — a career trajectory that gave her fluency in Arabic, Swahili, and the dialect of Washington that translates "bipartisan" as "I'll cross the aisle just often enough to make both sides nervous" [1]. She won Michigan's 8th District in 2018 by flipping a Republican seat, held it twice more, then leveled up to the Senate in 2024 by defeating Republican Mike Rogers with the kind of national-security bona fides that make attack ads difficult to write [2].

In the Senate, Slotkin has wasted no time establishing herself as the Democrat most likely to give her own caucus heartburn. She was one of twelve Democrats to vote for the Laken Riley Act, one of seven to confirm Kristi Noem as Homeland Security Secretary — a decision she later described with the diplomatic understatement that Noem had been "more of a bystander" to policy — and the lone Democrat to support killing California's gas-car phaseout, citing the million-plus Michiganders whose livelihoods depend on internal combustion [3][4]. Her willingness to break ranks has earned her the awkward distinction of being praised by Fox News and scolded by environmental groups in the same news cycle, which she appears to regard as evidence that she is doing something right.

On the issues that animate the 80% Bill, Slotkin's record is more complicated than her crossover votes suggest. She has championed PFAS cleanup legislation and co-led the bipartisan Great Lakes Restoration Initiative Act to boost funding to $500 million annually — positions that play well both at kitchen tables in Holly and in committee hearings [5]. She voted against the GOP's sweeping spending bill in 2025, citing cuts to infrastructure, veterans' services, and Great Lakes protections, proving she is perfectly capable of opposing Republicans when the math involves Michigan jobs rather than culture-war litmus tests [6]. Her support for Medicare drug-price negotiation and opposition to dark money in politics align with broadly popular positions, though her corporate PAC relationship has attracted the kind of scrutiny that makes "no corporate PAC" pledges a recurring punchline on her OpenSecrets page [7].

Slotkin's theory of the case is that voters want a senator who will pick up the phone when a Cabinet secretary calls, even if that secretary was confirmed with help from across the aisle. Whether this constitutes principled pragmatism or strategic triangulation depends on which side of the aisle you ask, but the voting public in Michigan — a state that swung for Trump in 2016, Biden in 2020, and Slotkin in 2024 — appears to have a high tolerance for politicians who refuse to stay in their lane [8]. In a chamber where most members treat party-line votes as a cardiovascular exercise, Slotkin remains committed to the unsettling proposition that senators should occasionally think before they vote.

Greg Stanton is a Democrat representing Arizona's 4th Congressional District, a seat he has held since 2019 after serving as mayor of Phoenix from 2012 to 2018. A Marquette University graduate who earned his law degree at the University of Michigan, Stanton built his political brand on pragmatic problem-solving, winning a Governing magazine "Public Official of the Year" nod in 2017 for expanding light rail and cutting greenhouse emissions [1]. He sits on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee, the Foreign Affairs Committee, and the Select Committee on Strategic Competition with China [2]. He is, on paper, exactly the kind of reform-minded Democrat the 80% Bill was written for.

And on a striking number of the twenty bills, Stanton delivers. He has been a leading voice on banning congressional stock trading, co-introducing the bipartisan TRUST in Congress Act in both 2023 and 2025, declaring that "Americans need to know their elected leaders in Washington are making decisions based on what's best for our country, not their own stock portfolios" [3][4]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, calling it "a hard-fought victory" he had been working toward for years [5]. He voted for the For the People Act (HR 1), the sweeping democracy reform package that addressed gerrymandering, dark money disclosure, and campaign finance reform, which every House Democrat co-sponsored [6]. On police body cameras, Stanton has been a genuine champion, introducing the COPS Accountability Act requiring body cameras as a condition of federal COPS grants and authoring legislation mandating body-worn cameras for Capitol Police after January 6th, the latter of which actually passed the House [7][8]. He also backed legislation to counter China's influence in U.S. markets and protect American entrepreneurs from state-sponsored enterprises [9].

But the gap between rhetoric and reality has an awkward habit of showing up in disclosure filings. In September 2023, while Stanton was publicly crusading to ban congressional stock trading, his wife's new investment firm purchased 94 individual stocks including shares in Chevron, ExxonMobil, ConocoPhillips, Philip Morris, Qualcomm, and NVIDIA, effectively mimicking the S&P 500 [10][11]. Stanton says he learned of the trades on October 21 and ordered them divested two days later, blaming the new firm for not knowing about his "no individual stocks" policy. An anti-corruption watchdog noted the obvious: it was "surprising" that the investment team didn't have clear instructions from the start [10]. It is a peculiar kind of irony when the man drafting the stock-trading ban has to file emergency disclosures because nobody told his wife's broker about the stock-trading ban.

Follow the money and the picture gets murkier. Stanton's campaign finance profile on OpenSecrets shows contributions from the securities and investment industry, the real estate sector, and the lawyers and lobbyists world, the very industries many of the 80% Bill's provisions target [12]. He serves on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee, a perch with enormous influence over federal contracts and development spending, which makes those real estate and construction contributions worth watching [2][12]. To his credit, Stanton has not been flagged as a major corporate PAC beneficiary in the way some colleagues have, and his overall record on democracy reform is well above average for a House member. But the stock episode is a reminder that even the most vocal reformers sometimes need the very guardrails they are trying to build.

The bottom line: Greg Stanton aligns with a solid majority of the 80% Bill, particularly on stock trading bans, Medicare negotiation, body cameras, and democracy reform. He talks a good game on clean government, and in most cases he backs it up with votes and legislation. The stock trading misadventure with his wife's portfolio does not make him a hypocrite so much as a case study in why the ban he champions actually needs to pass. If your own household cannot navigate the honor system, maybe it is time to stop relying on one.

Pete Stauber's biography reads like a casting director's dream for a Republican congressman from Minnesota's Iron Range: hockey champion, wounded cop, pro-mining crusader. He captained Lake Superior State University to a Division I national hockey championship in 1988, played three seasons in the Detroit Red Wings' minor league system, then retired with a neck injury and joined the Duluth Police Department, where he spent 23 years and survived being shot in the head while off-duty and having a suspect's gun misfire at point-blank range [1]. If that sounds like the setup for an action movie, the sequel is less cinematic: Stauber won Minnesota's 8th District in 2018 and has since devoted his congressional career to ensuring that a Chilean mining conglomerate can dig copper and nickel next to one of America's most pristine wilderness areas.

Stauber chairs the House Subcommittee on Energy and Mineral Resources, a post he has leveraged with missionary zeal to advance the Twin Metals copper-nickel mine near the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness [2]. The mine is owned by Antofagasta, a Chilean company controlled by the billionaire Luksic family, and Stauber introduced legislation granting the company 20-year mineral leases with mandatory 50-year renewals — a sweetheart deal that prompted the Minnesota Reformer to ask what explains his "slavish devotion to a Chilean mining company" [3]. Environmental groups note that copper-nickel sulfide mining has a 100% track record of polluting waterways, and the Boundary Waters draws over 150,000 visitors annually, but Stauber has framed opposition to the mine as an "attack on our way of life" [4]. In January 2026, he pushed House Joint Resolution 140 through Congress to end the Biden-era mining moratorium in Superior National Forest, effectively clearing the path for Twin Metals to proceed.

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Stauber occupies an awkward middle ground between his union-friendly roots and his party-line voting record. He boasts a "100 percent voting record" on prevailing wage and right-to-work issues, winning endorsements from ten unions in 2020 including boilermakers, carpenters, and LIUNA [5]. Yet he voted against the bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act in 2021 — then toured his district touting the airport grants, broadband funding, and Duluth bridge improvements the bill paid for, a performance that earned him bipartisan ridicule and a Huffington Post headline noting he was praising infrastructure "paid for by the bill he voted against" [6]. His AFL-CIO scorecard tells the fuller story: on the big votes that define labor's legislative agenda, Stauber reliably sides with the Republican caucus.

Stauber's relationship with democratic norms follows a similarly bifurcated pattern. He voted to certify the 2020 election results on January 6, 2021, breaking with 139 House Republicans — but only after signing onto Texas v. Pennsylvania in December 2020, the lawsuit that sought to invalidate millions of votes in swing states and that the Supreme Court dismissed as lacking standing [7]. He voted against the bill clarifying the ceremonial role of Congress and the vice president in certifying elections, apparently preferring to keep that ambiguity available for future use. His voting record with Trump's stated positions sits at roughly 90%, and his opposition to climate legislation, gun safety measures, and the Inflation Reduction Act places him squarely in the party mainstream [8]. For the Iron Range voters who remember when their congressman was a union cop who played hockey, the transformation into a reliable vote for tax cuts and deregulation has been less a betrayal than a reminder that in modern Republican politics, the blue collar is mostly for show.
